questions.question: Give an example of a ‘liquid in solid’ type solution.
questions.answer: Liquid in solid means solvent will be solid and the solute will be liquid. Example, mercury in sodium (amalgam).

questions.question: Which type of solid solution will result by mixing two solid components with large differences in the sizes of their molecules?
questions.answer: A solid solution which will result by mixing two solid components with large difference in the sizes of their molecules is known as interstitial solid solution.

questions.question: What is meant by semimolar and decimolar solutions?
questions.answer: The semimolar and decimolar terms are completely based on the molarity of the solution. Semimolar signifies the molarity of solution as M 2 and decimolar signifies the molarity of solution as M 10 .

questions.question: What will be the mole fraction of water in 2 5 C H OH solution containing equal number of moles of water and 2 5 C H OH?
questions.answer: Mole fraction of any component is given as the ratio of the respective moles of the same to the total moles of the solution. Here, if we consider x moles of water then we will have equal i.e., x moles of 2 5 C H OH . Thus, 2 x 1 Mole fraction = 0.5 x x 2 = = +

questions.question: Which of the following is a dimensionless quantity: molarity, molality or mole fraction?
questions.answer: Out of molarity, molality and mole fraction; mole fraction is the dimensionless quantity as it is the ratio of similar quantities i.e., moles.

questions.question: 10 g glucose is dissolved in 400 g of solution. Calculate percentage concentration of the solution.
questions.answer: In general, the percentage is the ratio of a component to the total solution multiplied by 100. Thus, the percentage concentration of the solution is given as,

questions.question: Gases tend to be less soluble in liquids as the temperature is raised. Why?
questions.answer: When gases are dissolved in liquid, they have high velocities due to their free movement; rising the temperature shifts the equilibrium of the reaction in the backward direction which then makes gases less soluble in liquid.

questions.question: State the conditions which must be satisfied if an ideal solution is to be formed.
questions.answer: If an ideal solution is to be formed, the primary condition to be fulfilled will be a solution obeying Raoult’s law. Also, the solution should be homogenous and there must not be any volumetric or thermal effects.

questions.question: A mixture of chlorobenzene and bromobenzene forms a nearly ideal solution but a mixture of chloroform and acetone does not. Why?
questions.answer: In the case of a mixture of chlorobenzene and bromobenzene, there is equal interaction of the molecules hence, forming an ideal solution. Whereas, they are absent in the mixture of chloroform and acetone.

questions.question: How is the concentration of a solute present in trace amount in a solution expressed?
questions.answer: When the solution has trace amounts of solute present in them, the concentration of the solute is expressed in parts per million i.e., ppm. 11. 2 N and 2 O gases have H K values 76.48 kbar and 34.86 kbar respectively at 293 K temperature. Which one of these will have more solubility in water? In general, lesser the value of H K greater the solubility of a gas at the same partial pressure. Here, we can see 2 O has less value of H K therefore, it will be more soluble than 2 N .

questions.question: Under what condition molality and molarity of a solution are identical. Explain with suitable reasons.
questions.answer: The condition where the molarity and molality of the solution will be identical, is the presence of high amounts of solvent in the same i.e., dilute solutions.

questions.question: Addition of 2 HgI to KI (aq.) shows an increase in vapor pressure. Why?
questions.answer: Vapor pressure is the colligative property which is completely dependent on the number of particles the solution has. When 2 HgI is added to KI, they form a complex decreasing the number of solute particles. Thus, the vapor pressure will increase.

questions.question: What will happen to the boiling point of the solution formed on mixing two 4 miscible liquids showing negative deviation from Raoult’s law?
questions.answer: When the two miscible liquids showing negative deviation from Raoult’s law are mixed forming a solution, then the vapor pressure will decrease, increasing its boiling point.

questions.question: Liquid ‘Y’ has higher vapor pressure than liquid ‘X’, which of them will have higher boiling point?
questions.answer: In general, high vapor pressure corresponds to the low boiling point of the liquid. Here, the vapor pressure of liquid ‘X’ is the lowest thus, it will have the highest boiling point.

questions.question: When 50 mL of ethanol and 50 mL of water are mixed, predict whether the volume of the solution is equal to, greater than or less than 100 mL. Justify.
questions.answer: When the equal volume of ethanol and water are mixed together i.e., 50 ml each then the total volume formed will be less than 100 ml. This is because the molecules of ethanol are smaller than that of water which makes it fit themselves within the big water molecules.

questions.question: Which type of deviation is shown by the solution formed by mixing cyclohexane and ethanol?
questions.answer: The solution formed by mixing cyclohexane and ethanol will show positive deviation from Raoult’s law.

questions.question: A and B liquids on mixing produce a warm solution. Which type of deviation from Raoult’s law is there?
questions.answer: When mixing A and B produces a warm solution, then the solution will show negative deviation from Raoult’s law.

questions.question: Define cryoscopic constant (molal freezing point depression constant.)
questions.answer: The freezing point depression on dissolving a non – volatile solute in 1 Kg solvent is known as cryoscopic constant or molal freezing point depression constant.

questions.question: Mention the unit of ebullioscopic constant (molal boiling point elevation constant.)
questions.answer: The unit of ebullioscopic constant or molal boiling point elevation constant is 1 K kg mol−.

questions.question: If f K for water is 1 1.86Kkgmol− , what is the freezing point of 0.1 molal solution of a substance which undergoes no dissociation or association of solute?
questions.answer: The freezing point depression is given as, f f T iK m Δ = Here, i = 0 as there is no dissociation or association of solute. Thus, f f T K m Δ = fT 1.86 0.1 0.186K Δ = × =

questions.question: What is reverse osmosis? Give one large scale use of it.
questions.answer: The process takes place opposite to that of the osmosis (solvent flow towards solution through semi – permeable membrane) by applying pressure greater than osmotic pressure on the solution. The most common large scale application is desalination of seawater.

questions.question: What is the maximum value of van’t Hoff factor (i) for 2 4 2 Na SO .10H O ?
questions.answer: The maximum value of Van’t Hoff factor (i) for 2 4 2 Na SO .10H O will be 3.

questions.question: What is the value of van’t Hoff factor (i) if solute molecules undergo dimerisation.
questions.answer: The value of Van’t Hoff factor (i) for a solute who undergoes dimerization will be 0.5.

questions.question: Under what condition is van’t Hoff factor less than one?
questions.answer: Under association of molecules only, the Van’t Hoff factor will be less than 1.

questions.question: The Phase Diagram for pure solvent and the solution containing non – volatile solute are recorded below. The quantity indicated by ‘X’ in the figure is known as:
questions.answer: The quantity ‘X’ as indicated in the figure indicates b T Δ . 27. 3 AgNO reaction with NaCl in aqueous solution gives white precipitate. If the two solutions are separated by a semipermeable membrane, will there be the appearance of a white ppt. in the side ‘X’ due to osmosis? If the solutions of 3 AgNO and NaCl are separated by a semipermeable membrane; there will be no appearance of ppt due to the movement of only solvent particles through the membrane and not of solute particles. 7 Short Answer Questions

questions.question: Explain the following: (a) Solubility of a solid in a liquid involves dynamic equilibrium.
questions.answer: The solubility of solid in the liquid is the maximum concentration of solute to be dissolved in solvent. Any extra solute will lead to the precipitation of the dissolved solute which leads to the attainment of dynamic equilibrium. (b) Ionic compounds are soluble in water but are insoluble in nonpolar solvents. In general, we know the empirical rule of ‘like dissolves like’. Here, the ionic compounds are polar molecules which are soluble in water due to its polar nature.

questions.question: Give two examples each of a solution: (a) showing positive deviation from Raoult’s Law.
questions.answer: The mixture of (i) Ethanol and water. (ii) Acetone and ethanol. (b) showing negative deviation from Raoult’s Law. The mixture of (i) HCl and water. (ii) Acetone and chloroform.

questions.question: Draw vapor pressure vs composition (in terms of mole fraction) diagram for an ideal solution.
questions.answer: Class XII Chemistry

questions.question: Define azeotropes with one example of each type.
questions.answer: The binary mixtures of solutions that have the same composition in liquid and vapor phase having constant boiling points throughout the distillation are known as azeotropes. (i) Minimum boiling azeotropes – mixture of water and ethanol (solutions showing positive deviation from Raoult’s law). (ii) Maximum boiling azeotropes – mixture of water and HCl (solutions showing negative deviation from Raoult’s law).

questions.question: Draw the total vapor pressure vs. mole fraction diagram for a binary solution exhibiting non-ideal behavior with negative deviation.
questions.answer: Class XII Chemistry

questions.question: The vapor pressure curve for three solutions having the same non- volatile solute in the same solvent are shown. The curves are parallel to each other and 9 do not intersect. What is the correct order of the concentrations of the solutions?
questions.answer: A < B < C As shown in the figure, the concentration of the three solutions having the same non – volatile solute in the same solvent will be in the order of A < B < C.

questions.question: Show that the relative lowering of vapor pressure of a solvent is a colligative property.
questions.answer: Colligative properties depend upon the number of solute particles in the solution. To be more precise, it is the ratio of the number of solute particles to the number of solvent particles in the solution. Now, relative lowering of vapour pressure is given as; 0 0 0 P P P P P Δ − = Also, by Raoult’s law we know that, 0 0 P P x P − = where, x is the mole fraction of any component. Hence, we can say that the relative lowering of vapor pressure of a solvent is a colligative property.

questions.question: What is meant by abnormal molecular mass? Illustrate it with suitable examples.
questions.answer: The molecular masses which are different theoretical molecular masses due to either association or dissociation of molecules in the solution is known as abnormal molecular masses. For example, weak electrolytes which dissociates to form ions.

questions.question: When 1 mole of NaCl is added to 1 liter water, the boiling point increases? When 1 mole of 3 CH OH is added to 1 liter water, the boiling point decreases? Suggest reasons.
questions.answer: When NaCl (non – volatile solute) is added to water then, the vapor pressure of the solution is lowered, increasing the boiling point. Whereas, 3 CH OH is more volatile than water i.e., has more vapor pressure than that of water decreasing the boiling point.

questions.question: Can we separate water completely from 3 HNO solution by vaporization? Justify your answer.
questions.answer: Water cannot be separated from nitric acid by evaporation or vaporization due to the formation of azeotropic mixture which makes the separation impossible.

questions.question: 1 gram each of two solutes ‘A’ and ‘B’ (molar mass of A > molar mass of B) are dissolved separately in 100 g each of the same solvent. Which solute will show greater elevation in boiling point and why?
questions.answer: The elevation in boiling point is given as, b b b n T K m K W Δ = = where, n = number of moles of a component given as, mass of component n molar mass of component = W = weight of solvent in Kgs. questions.question: Examine the following illustrations and answer the following questions 12 (a) Identify the liquid A and liquid B (pure water or sugar solution).
questions.answer: Liquid A is a sugar solution and liquid B is pure water. (b) Name the phenomenon involved in this experiment so that the level of liquid in this funnel has risen after some time. The ongoing phenomenon is the osmosis process in which the level of liquid in the funnel has risen after some time.

questions.question: How relative lowering in vapor pressure is related with depression in freezing point and elevation in boiling point?
questions.answer: The relative lowering in vapor pressure, depression in freezing point and elevation in boiling point; all the three are the colligative properties of the solutions and inter – related. The colligative properties completely depend upon the solute particles dissolved in solvent. So, when the non – volatile solute is added to the solvent due to change in vapor pressure, all other properties change. The relative lowering in vapor pressure of solute brings depression in freezing point and elevation in boiling point of the same. Short Answer Questions

questions.answer: A gas law which states that the amount of gas dissolved in liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the same gas above the liquid level when the temperature is kept constant is known as Henry’s law. questions.question: Given reason for the following: – (a) Aquatic species are more comfortable in cold waters than in warm waters.
questions.answer: The solubility of oxygen decreases with increase in temperature which makes aquatic organisms feel comfortable in cold water due to the high amount of dissolved oxygen. (b) To avoid bends scuba divers use air diluted with helium. 14 The bends during scuba diving is a condition which is very painful and dangerous for life. Thus, divers use helium as a dissolved gas in the air due to its low solubility in human blood. (c) Cold drinks bottles are sealed under high pressure of 2 CO . The 2 CO gas is filled in the cold drinks under high pressure and sealed as with high pressure, the solubility of gases in liquid increases.

questions.question: Why should a solution of a non-volatile and non-electrolyte solute boil at a higher temperature? Explain with the help of a diagram. Derive the relationship between molar mass and elevation in boiling point.
questions.answer: The solution of a non – volatile and non – electrolyte solute should boil at high temperature as their addition lowers the vapor pressure causing them to boil only at higher temperature i.e., higher than the boiling point of pure solvent. This can be explained by the below given diagram; The relationship between the molar mass of solute and the elevation in boiling point is given as; Elevation in boiling point, b b T K m Δ = where, m = molality and is stated as; 2 2 1 W m M W = 15 Now, putting the values in empirical equation we get, b 2 2 b 1 K W M T W =Δ

questions.question: Account for the following: – (a) 2 CaCl is used to clear snow from roads in hill stations.
questions.answer: The calcium chloride is responsible for depression in freezing point of water to an extent from where it cannot form ice hence, clearing the snow from roads. (b) Ethylene glycol is used as an antifreeze solution in radiators of vehicles in cold countries. The addition of ethylene glycol in car radiators lowers the freezing point of water so as to avoid bursting of lines in cold winter temperatures. (c) The freezing point depression of 0.01 m NaCl is nearly twice that of 0.01 m glucose solution. The sodium chloride dissociates in the solution giving two ions and glucose will never dissociate to form ions. Thus, by the relationship of freezing point depression; f f T iK m Δ = NaCl can have nearly twice the depression in freezing point to that of glucose even though the concentrations are the same.

questions.question: Why do colligative properties of solution of a given concentration are found to give abnormal molecular weight of solute. Explain with the help of suitable examples.
questions.answer: The colligative properties have been found to give abnormal molecular masses due to the presence of fact about association or dissociation of solute molecules in 16 solvent. To help this out Van’t Hoff factor was introduced which properly explained the fact. Solute undergoing association in the solution, Van’t Hoff factor is less then 1. Whereas, solute undergoing dissociation in the solution has Van’t Hoff factor more than 1. Example: KCl K Cl + − + Here, a molecule of KCl gives 2 ions which gives Van’t Hoff factor (i) as 2. This will reflect in the form of abnormal molecular mass calculations for certain conditions.

questions.question: Give reasons for the following: – (a) RBC swells up and finally bursts when placed in 0.1% NaCl solution.
questions.answer: When the RBCs are placed in the 0.1% NaCl solution the water will flow inside the cells as it moves from less concentrated to more concentrated solution. At a point the cell will be so swollen, enough to burst. (b) When fruits and vegetables that have been dried are placed in water, they slowly swell and return to original form. The drying of fruits or vegetables causes them to become more concentrated. When these are placed in water, the phenomenon of osmosis takes place resulting in the original form of those fruits and vegetables. (c) A person suffering from high blood pressure is advised to take less amount of table salt. The intake of NaCl can result in an increase in the number of solutes in the body fluids. If the solute concentration is increased, the osmotic pressure increment can rupture the blood cells.

questions.question: Glycerin, ethylene glycol and methanol are sold at the same price per kg. Which would be cheaper for preparing an antifreeze solution for the radiator of an automobile?
questions.answer: The antifreeze solution is responsible for lowering the freezing point of water so as to avoid the damage in the radiator in automobile system. Here, 3 solutes are needed to be compared i.e., glycerin, ethylene glycol and methanol. According the formula; f f T K m Δ = where, m = molality and is stated as; 2 2 1 W m M W = We can say that the lower molar mass will give depression in freezing point and hence, the best results at lower amounts. Molar masses are given as; Glycerin = 92 g/mol Ethylene glycol = 62 g/mol Methanol = 32 g/mol Thus, methanol will be cheaper for preparing an antifreeze solution for the radiator of an automobile.

questions.question: Determine the correct order of the property mentioned against them: (a) 10% glucose ( 1 p ), 10% urea ( 2 p ), 10% sucrose ( 3 p ) [Osmotic pressure]
questions.answer: 10% urea > 10% glucose > 10% sucrose. 18 As, the osmotic pressure depends upon the molar concentration of the solute; urea has the highest molar concentration i.e., more moles in the solution than that of glucose and sucrose. (b) 0.1 m NaCl, 0.1 m urea, 0.1 m 2 MgCl [Elevation in b.pt.] 0.1 m 2 MgCl > 0.1 m NaCl > 0.1 urea. As, the elevation in boiling point depends upon the Van’t Hoff factor; 2 MgCl will dissociate producing the highest number of ions (3) than that of NaCl (2) and urea(0). (c) 0.1 m 2 CaCl , 0.1 m sucrose, 0.1 m NaCl [Depression in f.pt.] 0.1 m 2 CaCl > 0.1 m NaCl > 0.1 m sucrose. As, the depression in freezing point depends upon the Van’t Hoff factor; 2 CaCl will dissociate producing the highest number of ions (3) than that of NaCl (2) and sucrose(0).

questions.answer: The solutions obeying Raoult’s law at any concentration and temperature are known as ideal solutions. For example; (i) Benzene and toluene (ii) Ethyl bromide and ethyl iodide. questions.question: (a) Why is the freezing point depression considered as a colligative property?
questions.answer: The colligative properties depend upon the number of solute molecules in the solvent. The depression in freezing point is observed when the solute particles are introduced in the solution. Thus, it is one of the colligative properties. (b) The cryoscopic constant of water is 1.86 k/m. Comment on this statement. If it is said that the cryoscopic constant of water is 1 1.86Km−, it means that the water has 1.86 K depression in freezing point when the solution has molality of 1 m. questions.question: (a) Define osmotic pressure.
questions.answer: The pressure that will prevent the process of osmosis is known as osmotic pressure. It is defined as the pressure applied at the side of pure solvent to prevent it to pass through semi – permeable membrane to the solution side by osmosis. (b) Why osmotic pressure is preferred over other colligative properties for the determination of molecular masses of macromolecules? The method of osmotic pressure takes into account the molarity of the solution unlike other methods who take molality as a multiplying factor. Also, the pressure measurement takes place at room temperature during osmosis. (c) What is the molar concentration of particles in human blood if the osmotic pressure is 7.2 atm at normal body temperature of 37 C ° ? 24 Given that, 7.2atm π= 1 1 R 0.0821LatmK mol − − = T 37 C 310K = ° = The osmotic pressure is given as; CRT π= The molar concentration is given as; C RT π = 7.2 C 0.283M 0.082 310 = = × NUMERICAL PROBLEMS

questions.question: Why is it not possible to measure single electrode potential?

questions.question: Electrolysis of KBr(aq) gives 2 Br at anode but does not give 2 F . Give a reason.
questions.answer: Oxidation takes place at anode. Now higher the oxidation Potential, easier to oxidize. Oxidation potential of Br− , 2 H O ,F− are in the following order. 2 Br H O F − − > > . Therefore in aq Solution of KBr. Br− ions are oxidized to 2 Br in preference to 2 H O . On the other hand, in aqueous solution of KF, 2 H O is oxidized in preference to F− . Thus in this case oxidation of 2 H O at anode gives 2 O and no 2F is produced.

questions.question: Define corrosion. Write the chemical formula of rust.
questions.answer: Corrosion is a process of determination of metal as a result of its reaction with air and water surrounding it. It is due to the formulation of sulphides, oxides, carbonates, hydroxides, etc. Formula of rust- 2 3 2 Fe O .xH O

questions.question: Write short notes on reduction and oxidation potentials.
questions.answer: oxidation potential: this is a measure of the tendency of a chemical species to acquire electrons loose or to get oxidized at an electrode. Reduction potential: this is a measure of the tendency of a chemical species to acquire electrons gain or to get reduced at an electrode.

questions.question: How are standard electrode potentials measured?
questions.answer: the standard electrode potential is measured by a galvanic cell which is constructed with a standard hydrogen electrode on one side and an unknown half cell on the other side.

questions.question: What is cell constant? How is it determined?
questions.answer: cell constant is defined as the ratio of distance between the electrodes which is divided by the area of the cross-sectional of the electrode. Calculating the resistance of a cell containing a known conductivity solution is the process of determining cell constant experimentally.

questions.question: What is conductivity water?
questions.answer: Conductivity water is a measure of the ability of water to pass an electric current.

questions.question: Why is it necessary to platinumize the electrodes of a conductivity cell before it is used for conductance measurement?
questions.answer: Because platinization will increase the surface area of the electrode on which the hydrogen gas can be contacted with hydrogen ions. Due to this the reactions happen faster and lead to better conduction measurement.

questions.question: Why does mercury cell give constant voltage?
questions.answer: Due to The overall cell reaction does not include any ion in the solution whose concentration changes during its life time.

questions.question: What is a fuel cell? Write reaction involved in 2 2 H O − fuel cell.
questions.answer: An electrochemical cell in which chemical energy of fuel is directly converted into electrical energy is known as Fuel cell. The Half cell reactions in Hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell as follows: Oxidation at anode: 2 2 2H (g) 4OH (aq) 4H O(l) 4e − − + → + Reduction at cathode: 2 2 O (g) 2H O(l) 4e 4OH (aq) − − + + →

questions.question: Why is Li the best reducing agent whereas Fluorine is the best oxidizing agent? but not to cell E . Explain.
questions.answer: according to the electrochemical series, Li ion has the lowest reduction potential which means it acts as the best reducing agent. Similarly, F has the highest reduction potential, thus fluorine oxidizes another substance readily. So, F is the best oxidizing agent.

questions.question: Why sodium metal is not obtained at cathode when NaCl is electrolysed with Pt electrodes but obtained when molten NaCl is electrolysed?
questions.answer: Because the reduction potential of Na+ , 0 Na /Na E 2.7V + =− is energetically more difficult than the reduction of water.

questions.answer: (a) Training is any process by which the aptitudes, skills and abilities of the employees to perform specific jobs are increased. It is a process of learning new skills and application of knowledge. It attempts to improve their performance on the current job or prepare them for any intended job. (b) Development refers to the learning opportunities designed to help employees grow. It covers not only those activities which improve job performance but also those which bring about growth of the personality, help individuals in the progress towards maturity and actualisation of their potential capacities so that they become not only good employees but better men and women.

questions.question: Why does an electrochemical cell stop working after some time?
questions.answer: The reduction potential of an electrode depends upon the concentration of solution with which it is in contact. As the cell works, the concentration of reactants decreases. Then according to Le Chatelier’s principle, it will shift the equilibrium in the backward direction. On the other hand, if the concentration is more on the reactant side then it will shift the equilibrium in the forward direction. When cell works concentration in an anodic compartment in cathodic compartment decreases and hence 0 cathode E cathode will decrease. Now EMF of the cell is 0 0 0 cell cathode anode E E – E = A decrease in 0 cathode E and a corresponding increase in 0 anode E wil mean that EMF of the cell will decrease and will ultimately become zero i.e., cell stops working after some time.

questions.question: What is understood by a normal hydrogen electrode? Give its significance.
questions.answer: Normal hydrogen electrode also known as Standard Hydrogen Electrode which is used for reference on half cell potential reactions. By using normal hydrogen electrodes, we can calculate cell potentials using different electrodes and this is a standard measurement of an electrode potential for the thermodynamic scale of redox potential.

questions.question: Define electrode potential. Why absolute value of the reduction potential of electrode cannot be determined?
questions.answer: Ans: The voltage or potential difference of a cell assembled from a standard hydrogen electrode is known as electrode potential for a given electrode. The potential is determined by using standard hydrogen electrodes as a reference and so absolute potential cannot.

questions.question: Explain the term electrolysis. Discuss briefly the electrolysis of i.molten NaCl
questions.answer: Electrolysis: this is a process of decomposition of a chemical compound in aqueous solutions or in molten state by a chemical change during electric current The electrolysis of molten NaCl when sodium chloride is melted above 0 801 C then two electrodes are inserted into the melt and electric current is passed through the molten salt. The following chemical reactions take place at the electrodes. 10 At cathode: Na e Na + − + → At anode: 2 1 Cl Cl e 2 − − → + the overall reaction is, 2 2NaCl 2Na(s) Cl (g) → + ii. aqueous sodium chloride solution Sodium chloride dissolves as Na+ and Cl− into water and producing sodium and hydrogen at respective electrodes. questions.question: State and explain Faraday’s laws of electrolysis. What is the electrochemical equivalent?
questions.answer: First Law of Electrolysis: the deposited mass of an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passing through it during electrolysis. W = zct Where, W = deposited mass on the electrode 11 c = current and t = time in sec If 1 amp current is passed through a solution in one second then the deposited mass of substance on the electrode is equal to its electrochemical equivalent. Here, z is known as the electrochemical equivalent. Second Law of Electrolysis: the deposited masses on the electrodes are directly proportional to their chemical equivalents when the same quantity of electricity is passed through the different electrolytic cells. Consider, 1 2 1 2 W ,W &E ,E be the amount of mass deposited and their chemical equivalents respectively. According to faraday’s second law of electrolysis, 1 1 W E α , and 2 2 W E α Then, 1 1 2 2 W E W E =

questions.question: What do you understand by ‘electrolytic conduction’? What are the factors on which electrolyte conduction depends? What is the effect of temperature on electrolytic conduction?
questions.answer: Ans: When electric current passes through the electrolytic solutions then the ability of the solutions to allow the current is known as electrolytic conductance. Factors affecting on electrolytic conductance, a. Concentration of ions b. Nature of electrolyte c. Temperature Effect of temperature: when temperature changes the electrolyte gets dissolved in solution. Hence, the temperature increases the solubility of electrolytes and increases the electrolytic conduction.

questions.question: How is electrolytic conductance measured experimentally?
questions.answer: the resistance between two nodes will give information about the conductance of electrolyte. When electricity passes through electrolyte solution dissociates into positive ions and negative ions. The conductance of electrolyte can be measured by using galvanic cells or the method of electrolysis.

questions.question: Describe normal hydrogen electrodes and its applications.
questions.answer: An electrode which is used for reference on all half cell potential reactions is known as normal hydrogen electrode or standard hydrogen electrode. questions.question: Iron does not rust even if Zinc coating is broken in a galvanised iron pipe but rusting occurs much faster if tin coating over iron is broken. Explain.
questions.answer: Fe is less electropositive than Zn. So, as long as Zn on the surface of Fe pipe then Zn acts as anode and Fe pipe acts as cathode. As a result of this, rusting of Fe is prevented by Zn. On the other hand, Zn is more electropositive than Sn, which protects iron until the Sn coating is unbroken. If any pores or breaks are observed, even tin is there the exposed iron gets rusted.

questions.question: Mention the factors that affect the rate of a chemical reaction.
questions.answer: The rate of a reaction is influenced by several things. (i) Reactant nature: The rate of the reaction is determined by the reactant's nature. Ionic compound reactions, for example, are faster than covalent compound reactions. (ii) Reactant state: Solid reactions are sluggish, liquid reactions are quick, and gas reactions are very fast. (iii) Temperature: The rate of reaction is heavily influenced by the temperature. Temperature raises the pace of reaction by 2-3 times for every 0 100 C increase in temperature. 10 2 3 T T r r + = − 11 The temperature coefficient is the name given to this proportion. The average kinetic energy of reactant molecules increases as the temperature rises. As a result, the rate of collisions rises. The number of molecules with threshold energy grows as the temperature rises. As a result, the rate of reaction accelerates. iv) Catalyst presence: The rate of reaction also is affected by the presence of a catalyst. Catalysts boost reaction rates by increasing reaction surface area, generating an unstable intermediate with the substrate, and providing a lower-activation-energy alternative path.

questions.question: Write the electronic configuration of 3 + Cr ion (atomic number of Cr = 24 )
questions.answer: The atomic number of chromium is 24, hence the electronic configuration of 3 + Cr will be 2 2 6 2 6 0 3 1s 2s 2p 3s 3p 4s 3d .

questions.question: Explain 4 2 CuSO .5H Ois blue while 4 ZnSO and 4 CuSO are colourless?
questions.answer: Water acts as a ligand in 4 2 CuSO .5H O, causing crystal field splitting. As a result, in 4 2 CuSO .5H O, a d-d transition is possible and visible. Due to the lack of water in anhydrous 4 CuSO and 4 ZnSO , ligand crystal field splitting is not feasible, and hence no colour is produced.

questions.question: Why is the third ionisation energy of Manganese (Z = 25) is unexpectedly high?
questions.answer: Manganese has an atomic number of 25 and an electronic configuration of [ ] 2 5 Ar 4s ,3d . The loss of two electrons transforms 2 + Mn into [ ] 5 Ar 3d , a structure with a half-filled d-orbital that is exceedingly stable. As the third electron must be removed from the stable configuration of 2 + Mn , the third ionisation energy is extremely large.

questions.question: Which elements among 3d - transition elements, exhibit the highest oxidation state?
questions.answer: The electronic configuration of manganese is [ ] 2 5 Ar 4s ,3d . Class 12 Chemistry 2 In the d-subshell of manganese, there are the most unpaired electrons ( 5 electrons). As a result, manganese has the most oxidation states, spanning from 2 + to 7 + .

questions.question: Silver (Ag) has completely filled d-orbitals 10 (4d ) in its ground state. How can you say that it is a transition element?
questions.answer: In its ground state, silver possesses a totally filled 4d-orbital ( ) 10 1 4d ,5s .The oxidation states of silver are now 1 + and 2 + . An electron is removed from the s- orbital in the 1 + oxidation state. The d-orbital, on the other hand, loses one electron in the 2 + oxidation state. As a result, the d-orbital is now incomplete 9 (4d ). As a result, it's a transitional element.

questions.question: In 3d series → (Sc Zn), the enthalpy of atomisation of Zn is low. Why?
questions.answer: The enthalpy of atomization is determined by the amount of metallic bonding that an element has. An element's enthalpy of atomization increases as its metallic bonding becomes more extensive. There are some unpaired electrons in all transition metals (except zinc, electronic configuration: 10 2 3d , 4s ), which explain for their stronger metallic bonding. The inter-atomic electrical bonding in zinc is the weakest due to the absence of these unpaired electrons, and as a result, it has the lowest enthalpy of atomization.

questions.question: Out of the following elements, identify the element which does not exhibit variable oxidation state? Chromium(Cr), Cobalt(Co), Zinc(Zn)
questions.answer: The only element which does not exhibit variable oxidation states, among the above given elements is zinc.

questions.question: The +3 oxidation state of lanthanum(Z = 57) , gadolinium(Z = 64) and lutetium(Z = 71) are especially stable. Why? Class 12 Chemistry
questions.answer: Lanthanum(57) 1 2 0 5d ,6s ,4f , Lutetium(71) 14 1 2 4f ,5d ,6s , and Gadolinium(64) 7 1 2 4f ,5d ,6s have the same electrical configuration. As a result, they have an empty, fully filled, and half-filled 4f -subshell, and their 3 +oxidation state is stable.

questions.question: Mention one consequence of Lanthanoid Contraction?
questions.answer: In the lanthanoids, from ( )3 La OH to ( )3 Lu OH , the basic strength of hydroxides drops. The size of 3 + M ions reduces as a result of lanthanoid contraction, and the covalent character of the M OH − bond increases.

questions.question: The first ionization enthalpies of 5d – series elements is higher than those of 3d and 4d series elements why?
questions.answer: Because of weak shielding by4f electrons, the first ionisation energy of5d elements is higher than that of3d elements. This is owing to the greater effective nuclear charge acting on outer valence electrons.

questions.question: Why 2 + Mn compounds are more stable than 2 + Fe compounds towards oxidation to their +3 state?
questions.answer: The electronic configuration of 2 + Mn is 5 [18Ar]3d and the electronic configuration of 2 + Fe is 6 [18Ar]3d . As 2 + Mn has a stable half-filled electronic structure, 2 + Mn compounds are more resistant to oxidation to their 3 + state than 2 + Fe compounds. 2 + Fe 6 (3d ) can easily lose one electron to become 3 + Fe ( 5 3d , stable configuration).

questions.question: Calculate the magnetic moment of 2 + Cu (Z =29) on the basis of “spin-only” formula.
questions.answer: The formula for calculating the magnetic moment is: = n(n + 2) B.M μ , where n is the number of unpaired electrons. Class 12 Chemistry 4 The electronic configuration of 2 + Cu is 18 10 [Ar] 3d . Hence, it has only one unpaired electron. So, its magnetic moment will be: = n(n + 2) B.M μ = 1(1 + 2) = 1.73 B.M μ

questions.question: What is the shape of chromate ions?
questions.answer: The shape of chromate ions is tetrahedral.

questions.question: Why does vanadium pentoxide act as a catalyst?
questions.answer: Vanadium is a transition metal that quickly switches between oxidation states. It can create unstable intermediates in one oxidation state and then quickly convert to products by gaining a new stable oxidation state, opening up a new reaction pathway.

questions.question: What are interstitial compounds?
questions.answer: Interstitial compounds are formed when very small atoms, such as hydrogen, nitrogen, and carbon, become trapped inside the crystal lattices of metals. These chemicals are usually non-stoichiometric and not ionic or covalent.

questions.question: The transition metals and their compounds are known for their catalytic activity. Give two specific reasons to justify the statement.
questions.answer: Transition metals have a wide range of oxidation states and are capable of forming complexes. They produce unstable intermediates. They open up a novel pathway with lower reaction activation energy. They also provide an appropriate surface for the reaction to take place on. Class 12 Chemistry

questions.question: Mention the name and formula of the ore from which potassium dichromate is prepared.
questions.answer: Potassium dichromate is prepared from an ore named chromite, and its formula is 2 4 FeCr O .

questions.question: Write the electronic configuration of 3 + Lu (At. No. =71).
questions.answer: The electronic configuration of 3 + Lu is 14 1 2 [Xe]4f 5d 6s

questions.question: What is the most common oxidation state of actinoids?
questions.answer: 3 + is the most common oxidation state achieved by actinoids.

questions.question: Write the names of the catalyst used in the: (a) Manufacture of sulphuric acid by contact process.
questions.answer: Vanadium oxide is the catalyst used in the manufacture of sulphuric acid by contact process. (b) Manufacture of polythene. Ziegler Nata catalyst which is the combination of titanium tetrachloride and trimethyl aluminium is used in the manufacture of polythene. Class 12 Chemistry

questions.question: Mention the name of the element among lanthanoids known to exhibit +4 oxidation state.
questions.answer: Cerium is the element in the lanthanoid series which is known to exhibit 4 + oxidation state

questions.question: Name one ore each of manganese and chromium.
questions.answer: The ore of manganese is pyrolusite and chromite is known as the ore of chromium.

questions.question: Why is 2 + Cd ion white?
questions.answer: Ans: 2 + Cd has 10 5d electrical structure. It signifies it has a full packed d-subshell. As a result, there will be no d-d transition, and hence, 2 + Cd will remain white.

questions.answer: Moving over a period, the basic character of transition metal oxides often declines. As a result, the proper sequence is:TiO VO CrO FeO > > > Short Answer Type Questions 2 Marks Class 12 Chemistry

questions.question: Write the chemical equation, when the yellow colour of aqueous solution of 2 4 Na CrO changes to orange on passing 2 CO gas?
questions.answer: The chemical equation is as follows: 2 2 CO 2 4 2 2 7 H O Na CrO Na Cr O → Sodium chromate is yellow in colour but when carbon dioxide gas is passed through it, it changes to sodium dichromate, which is orange in colour.

questions.question: The stability of 2 + Cu (aq) is more than that of + Cu (aq) . Why?
questions.answer: The hydration energy (enthalpy) of the ions when they connect to the water molecules determines their stability. 2 + Cu ions have a higher charge density than + Cu ions, forming much stronger interactions and releasing more energy as a result.

questions.question: Indicate the steps in the preparation of 2 2 7 K Cr O (a) From Chromite ore 4 KMnO
questions.answer: The steps of preparations are: 2 2 7 K Cr O from Chromite ore: Potassium dichromate is prepared in three steps: i. Synthesis of sodium chromate from chromite ore: 2 4 2 3 2 2 4 2 3 2 4FeCr O + 8Na CO + 7O 8Na CrO + 2Fe O + 8CO → ii. Sodium chromate to sodium dichromate conversion + 2 4 2 2 7 2 2Na CrO + 2H Na Cr O + 2Na + H O +→ iii. questions.question: Give reason for: (a) In permanganate ions, all bonds formed between manganese and oxygen are covalent.
questions.answer: Ans: 4 MnO −in the highest oxidation state, 7 + . Transition metals produce covalent bonds in high oxidation states (according to Fajan's rules, as oxidation state increases, ionic character decreases). (b) Permanganate titrations in presence of hydrochloric acid are unsatisfactory. Because hydrochloric acid is oxidised to chlorine, permanganate titrations in the presence of the acid are unreliable. In preparative organic chemistry, this is a preferred oxidant.

questions.answer: Ans: 2 + 2 - 3 + 3 + 2 7 2 6Fe + Cr O + 14H 6Fe + 2Cr + 7H O +→ (b) Oxidation of 2 + Mn by - 4 MnO in neutral or faintly alkaline medium. Ans: - 2 - 2 - - 4 2 3 2 2 4 8MnO + 3S O + H O 8MnO + 6SO + 2OH →

questions.question: Answer the following: (a) Why do transition metals show high melting points?
questions.answer: Transition metals have a high density, as well as high melting and boiling temperatures. Metallic bonding via delocalized d electrons causes these characteristics, which leads to increased cohesion as the number of shared electrons grows. (b) Out ofFeandCu, which one would exhibit higher melting point? The melting point of Fe is greater than that ofCu. This is due to the fact that iron contains four unpaired electrons in the 3d -subshell, but copper only has one Class 12 Chemistry 9 electron in the 4s -subshell. As a result, iron’s metallic connections are significantly stronger than that of copper’s.

questions.question: Describe giving reason which one of the following pairs has the property indicated: (a) 2 + Cr or 2 + Fe (stronger reducing agent).
questions.answer: Ans: 2 + Cr is a more powerful reducer than 2 + Fe . The conventional electrode potential values ( ) 3 + 2 + E Cr /Cr = --0.41 V ° and ( ) 3 + 2 + E Fe /Fe = + 0.77 V ° can be used to explain this. As a result, 2 + Cr is quickly oxidised to 3 + Cr , but 2 + Fe is not as easily oxidised to 3 + Fe . (b) 2 + Co or 2 + Ni (lower magnetic moments). The electronic configuration of 2 + Co is 2 2 6 2 6 7 1s 2s 2p 3s 3p 3d and the electronic configuration of 2 + Ni is 2 2 6 2 6 8 1s 2s 2p 3s 3p 3d . Hence, the magnetic moment of 2 + Ni lower than 2 + Co , because unpaired electrons are less in the valence shell of 2 + Ni than 2 + Co .

questions.question: Of the ions 2 + 3 + 3 + Co , Sc , Cr which one will give colourless aqueous solution and how will each of them respond to magnetic field and why?
questions.answer: The electronic configurations of 2 7 3 0 3 3 Co :[Ar]3d ; Sc :[Ar]3d ; Cr :[Ar]3d + + + . Unpaired electrons exist in 2 + Co and 3 + Cr . They are so coloured in an aqueous solution. 3 + Sc does not have any unpaired electrons. As a result, it is colourless.

questions.question: Complete the following equations: (a) → 2 2 MnO + KOH + O
questions.answer: The complete equation is as followed: 2 2 4 2 MnO + KOH + O KMnO + H O → (b) → 2 2 7 Na Cr O + KCl Class 12 Chemistry 10 The complete equation is as followed: 2 2 7 2 2 2 Na Cr O + 2KCl K Cr O + 2NaCl →

questions.question: Transition metals show low oxidation states with carbon monoxide.
questions.answer: Synergic bonds enable the formation of complexes by transition elements in zero oxidation states. In metal carbonyl, the metal-carbon bond has both s and p character. The M-C sigma bond is created when a lone pair of electrons from the carbonyl carbon are donated to a metal's empty orbital. The M-C bond is created when a pair of electrons from a metal's full d orbital are donated to carbon monoxide's unoccupied anti bonding pi* orbital. The synergic action of metal to ligand bonding enhances the connection between carbon monoxide and the metal.

questions.question: For the first row transition metals the enthalpy of atomisation value are: Sc Ti V Cr Mn Fe Co Ni Cu Zn o - 1 a Δ H / kJ mol 326 473 515 397 281 416 425 430 339 26 Assign reason for the following: (a) Transition elements have higher values of enthalpies of atomisation.
questions.answer: The reasons for the above statements are: Transition elements contain a large number of valence electrons and a high effective nuclear charge. As a result of the existence of an unpaired electron in the (n 1)d − -sub-shell, they form extremely strong metallic bonds with a high enthalpy of atomisation. (b) The enthalpy of atomisation of zinc is the lowest in 3d – series. Due to the lack of unpaired electrons and weak metallic connections, zinc has the lowest enthalpy of atomisation. Class 12 Chemistry

questions.question: Account for the following: (a) Copper shows its inability to liberate hydrogen gas from the dilute acids.
questions.answer: The reason behind it is that the Copper has a positive electrode potential, it cannot release hydrogen from acids. Hydrogen gas is liberated by metals with a negative electrode potential. So, Copper shows its inability to liberate hydrogen gas from the dilute acids. (b) Scandium (Z =21) does not exhibit variable oxidation states. In the ground state, Scandium (Z=21) possesses partially filled 3d -orbitals 1 (3d ) . As a result, it's classified as a transitional element.

questions.question: Copper (I) compounds undergo disproportionation. Write the chemical equation for the reaction involved and give reason.
questions.answer: The chemistry of copper(I) is restricted by a reaction that happens in solution involving simple copper(I) ions. This is an excellent illustration of disproportionation, which occurs when something oxidises and then reduces itself. Copper(I) ions in solution are disproportionately converted to copper(II) ions and a copper precipitate. The reaction is: + 2 + 2Cu (aq) Cu (aq) + Cu (s) →

So, titanium will lose three electrons, so that it will have one unpaired electron left. Thus, the oxidation number will be 3 +.

questions.question: Account for the following: (a) Transition metals and majority of their compounds act as good catalysts.
questions.answer: Transition metals and their compounds are effective catalysts because of their ability to exhibit changing oxidation states and form complexes, as well as the fact that they give a suitable surface for reaction to occur. For example, in the contact process, vanadium oxide is used, while in Haber's Process, finely split iron is used. Class 12 Chemistry 13 (b) From element to element, actinoid contraction is greater than lanthanoid contraction. Due to inadequate shielding by 5f -electrons in actinoids compared to 4f - electrons in lanthanoids, actinoid contraction is higher than lanthanoid contraction from element to element.

questions.question: Calculate the number of electrons transferred in each case when 4 KMnO acts as an oxidising agent to give (i) 2 MnO
questions.answer: The oxidation state of manganese in 4 KMnO is 7 +. The oxidation state of manganese in 2 MnO is 4 + . Hence, three electrons transfer will take place. (ii) 2 + Mn The oxidation state of manganese in 4 KMnO is 7 +. The oxidation state of manganese in 2 + Mn is 2 + . Hence, a transfer of five electrons will take place. (iii) 3 Mn(OH) The oxidation state of manganese in 4 KMnO is 7 +. The oxidation state of manganese in 3 Mn(OH) is 3 + . Hence, a transfer of four electrons will take place. (iv) 2- 4 MnO The oxidation state of manganese in 4 KMnO is 7 +. The oxidation state of manganese in 2 4 MnO −is 6 + . Hence, a transfer of one electron will take place.

questions.question: Account for the following: (a) 3 La(OH) is more basic than 3 Lu(OH)
questions.answer: The most basic is 3 La(OH) while the least basic is 3 Lu(OH) . The covalent nature of the hydroxides rises as the size of lanthanide ions falls from 3 + La to 3 + Lu , and therefore the basic strength lowers. (b) 2 + Zn salts are white. There are no unpaired electrons in the electronic arrangement of 2 + 10 Zn (3d ) salts. The presence of an unpaired electron in metal ions allows for electron transition in the visible range. This is why 2 + Zn ion salts are white in colour. (c) Cu (I) compounds are unstable in aqueous solution and undergo disproportionation. Ans: + Cu is more unstable in aqueous solution than 2 + Cu because, while copper's 2nd I.E. is considerable, 2 + Cu hydrations enthalpy is significantly lower than + Cu , and so it more than compensates for copper's 2nd I.E. As a result, many + Cu complexes in aqueous solution are unstable and disproportionate.

questions.question: Answer the following questions: (a) Deduce the number of 3d electrons in the following ions: 3 + Fe , 2 + Cu and 3 + Sc .
questions.answer: The electronic configuration of 3 + Fe is 2 2 6 2 6 5 1s 2s 2p 3s 3p 3d . So, 3 + Fe has5 electrons in 3d -orbital. The electron configuration of 2 + Cu is 2 2 6 2 6 9 1s 2s 2p 3s 3p 3d . So, 2 + Cu has9 electrons in3d -orbital. The electronic configuration of 3 + Sc is 2 2 6 2 6 0 1s 2s 2p 3s 3p 3d . So, 3 + Sc has no electrons in3d -orbitals. (b) Why do transition metals form alloys? Transition metals have atomic sizes that are extremely close to one another. Due to their comparable atomic sizes, one metal may easily replace the other in its lattice and create a solid solution, which is the alloy. This is why, in the molten state, transition metals create homogenous mixes with one another. (c) Write any two characteristics of interstitial compounds. The melting temperature of interstitial compounds is higher than that of pure metals, and they are tougher and more corrosion resistant.

questions.question: Name the catalysts used in the: (a) Manufacture of ammonia by Haber’s Process.
questions.answer: Iron is used as a catalyst in the Haber’s Process. (b) Oxidation of ethyne to ethanol. Palladium (II) chloride is used in the oxidation of ethyne to ethanol. (c) Photographic industry. Silver metal is used as a catalyst in photographic industry.

questions.question: Among 4 TiCl , 3 VCl and 2 FeCl , which one will be drawn more strongly into a magnetic field and why? Class 12 Chemistry
questions.answer: The transition metal ion with the most unpaired electrons will be significantly pulled into the magnetic field among the above given halides. The electronic configuration of 4 + Ti will be 2 2 4 2 6 0 1s 2s 2p 3s 3p 3d . It has non paired electrons in its outermost shell and hence its magnetic moment will be zero. The electronic configuration of 3 + V will be 2 2 4 2 6 2 1s 2s 2p 3s 3p 3d . It has two unpaired electronic in its d-orbital and hence its magnetic moment will be 2.76 B.M . The electronic configuration of 2 + Fe will be 2 2 4 2 6 6 1s 2s 2p 3s 3p 3d . It has four unpaired electrons and hence its magnetic moment will be 4.9 B.M. As the number of unpaired electrons in 2 + Fe are higher, its magnetic moment will also be greater. Hence, 2 FeCl will be drawn more strongly into a magnetic field.

questions.question: How do you account for the following? (a) With the same d-orbital configuration 4 (d ) , 2 + Cr is a reducing agent while 3 + Mn is an oxidising agent. Class 12 Chemistry
questions.answer: The configuration of 2 + Cr is 4 3d . 3 + Cr , which has a stable 3 3d structure, can lose one electron (as it has half -filled 2t glevel). As a result, it has a reducing property. 3 + Mn , on the other hand, has a 4 3d structure but can acquire an electron to become 2 + Mn , which has a stable 5 3d shape (as it is exactly half - filled). As a result, it is oxidising. (b) The actinoids exhibit a larger number of oxidation states than the corresponding members in the lanthanoid series. Because of the narrow energy gap between the5f , 6d , and 7ssubshells, actinides have greater oxidation states than lanthanides. As a result, the outermost electrons are easily stimulated to higher energy levels, resulting in oxidation states that are changeable. (c) Most of transition metal ions exhibit characteristic colours in aqueous solutions. The presence of unpaired d-electrons in transition metal ions in aqueous solution gives them their colour. These unpaired d-electrons are stimulated to a higher energy d-orbital of the same n value from a lower energy level of a metal ion in a complex. The frequency of light absorbed corresponds to the energy of excitation, and this frequency is visible. The colour seen is the complementary colour of the light that has been absorbed. The type of the ligand determines the frequency of light absorbed. Water molecules are ligands in aqueous solutions, and the colours of the ions detected are mentioned in the table below. Long Answer Type Questions 5 Marks

The orange solution when reacted with equi-molar ammonuim salt gives compound ‘D’ which when heated liberates nitrogen gas and compound ‘A’. Identify compounds A to D and write the chemical equation of the reactions involved.

questions.question: Assign reasons for the following: (a) There is no regular trends in E° values of 2 + M / M systems in 3d series.
questions.answer: Due to uneven electronic configurations from left to right, irregular change in ionisation enthalpies (sum of 1st and 2nd ionisation enthalpies), heat of sublimation, and enthalpy of hydration from left to right in the period. As a result, irregular variations in ( ) o 2 + E M /M values for ionisation metals are caused by irregular variations in ionisation enthalpies, and heat of sublimation and hydration enthalpy. (b) There is gradual decrease in the ionic radii of 2 + M ion in 3d series. The ionic radii get smaller as the nuclear charge gets higher. Because incoming electrons enter the inner (n 1)d − orbitals as nuclear charge rises, this reduction occurs. When the oxidation state goes from 2 + to 3 + , the ionic radii gradually decrease. (c) Majority of transition metals form complexes. Transition metals are the d-block elements having the outer shell electronic configuration as 2 1 - 10 ns (n 1)d − . These metal ions can easily form complexes with a group of negative ions or neutral molecules that have lone pairs of electrons. This is due to: the metals' small size and strong nuclear charge. Availability of empty d- orbitals with sufficient energy to accept ligand-donated lone pairs of electrons. (d) 3 + Ce can be easily oxidised to 4 + Ce The outer shell electronic configuration of 3 + Ce is 1 0 0 4f 5d 6s . 3 + Ce can easily lose one electron from the 4f -orbital and forms 4 + Ce with an outer shell electronic configuration of 0 0 0 4f 5d 6s . Thus, 3 + Ce gets easily oxidized to 4 + Ce . (e) Tantalum and palladium metals are used to electroplate coinage metals. Class 12 Chemistry 20 At a bare minimum, the coinage metals are those metallic chemical elements that have historically been utilised as components in coin alloys. Tantalum and palladium are valuable materials for electroplating coinage because of their chemical inertness.

questions.question: Account for the following: (a) Actinoids display a variety of oxidation states.
questions.answer: Actinides have a wide range of oxidation states, ranging from 3 + to 6 + . This is owing to the small energy gap between the subshells 5f , 6d , and 7s. The two main oxidation states are 3 + and 4 + , with 3 + being the most stable. In Thorium and Plutonium, the 4 + oxidation state is the most stable. (b) 2 + Yb behaves as a good reductant. Ytterbium (Yb) has the electrical configuration 14 2 4f 6s . Although Yb in the 2 + oxidation state acquires a fully filled 14 4f structure, it nevertheless oxidises to its 3 + states, making it a powerful reducing agent or reductant. (c) Cerium (IV) is a good analytical reagent. Cerium (IV), often known as 4 + Ce , is a useful analytical reagent. Because the electronics configuration of 4 + Ce is 0 [Xe]4f , a good analytical reagent is a chemical compound with a high standard of purity. 4 + Ce ions in solution are good oxidising agents because they tend to return to the more stable oxidation state of 3 +when an electron is lost or gained. 4 + 3 + [Ce /Ce ] has an Eo value of 1.74 V , indicating that it can oxidise water. Cerium (IV) has considerable kinetic stability, resulting in a very slow reaction rate, making it a useful analytical reagent. (d) Transition metal fluorides are ionic in nature while chlorides and bromides are covalent in nature. Fluorides of transition metals are ionic in nature, whereas bromides and chlorides are frequently covalent. According to Fajan's rule, the bigger the anion, the more polarizable it is, and thus the more covalent it is. The size of an atom grows as it moves along a group. Fluorides are hence smaller than chlorides and bromides. (e) Hydrochloric acid attacks all the actinoids. Class 12 Chemistry 21 Actinoids are more reactive than lanthanides; these metals are attacked by hydrochloric acid because no protective oxide layer forms on their surface, but only a little amount of nitric acid is added because a protective oxide layer forms on their surface.

questions.question: Explain by giving suitable reason: (a) Co(II) is stable in aqueous solution but in the presence of complexing agent it is readily oxidised.
questions.answer: Although Co(II) is stable in aqueous solutions, it is oxidised to Co(III)in the presence of strong field complexing agents. Despite the fact that Cobalt's third ionisation energy is large, the higher number of crystal field stability field ligands overcomes it. In addition, 3 + Co ions are more likely than 2 + Co ions to form coordination complexes. (b) 2 + Eu , 2 + Yb are good reductants whereas 4 + Tb is an oxidant. Europium's (Eu) electrical configuration is 7 2 4f 6s while Ytterbium's (Yb) is 14 2 4f 6s . In the 2 + oxidation state, Europium and Ytterbium form half-filled 7 (4f ) and fully-filled 14 (4f ) configurations, although they nevertheless oxidise to their typical 3 + states, acting as powerful reducing agents or reductants. Terbium possesses a 7 4f half-filled stable structure in the 4 + state, but it quickly reduces to the common 3 + state, and so it acts as an oxidant. (c) AgCl dissolves in ammonia solution. Because it forms 3 2 [Ag(NH ) ]Cl complexes, AgCl is soluble in ammonia. Because the complex + 3 2 [Ag(NH ) ] ion is formed, it behaves as an ionic ion, making it soluble. (d) Out of 2 + Cr or 2 + Fe , which one is a stronger reducing agent? Ans: 2 + Cr is a stronger reducing agent than 2 + Fe , due to the conventional electrode potential values ( ) 3 + 2 + E Cr /Cr = - 0.41 V o and ( ) 3 + 2 + E Fe /Fe = 0.77 V o which is higher for iron than chromium. As a result, 2 + Cr is quickly oxidised to 3 + Cr , whereas 2 + Fe is difficult to oxidise to 3 + Fe . (e) The highest oxidation state is exhibited in oxoanions of a transition metal Class 12 Chemistry 22 Metal oxoanions are covalent in nature, with oxygen participating in multiple bonding with the metal atom. The elevated oxidation state of the metal in oxoanions is due to this reason. For example, the element Mn has a 7 + oxidation state in permanganate ion.

questions.question: When a white crystalline compound A is heated with 2 2 7 K Cr O and conc. 2 4 H SO , a reddish brown gas B is evolved, which gives a yellow coloured solution C when passed through NaOH. On adding 3 CH COOH and 3 (CH COO)Pb to solution C, a yellow coloured ppt. D is obtained. Also on heating A with NaOHand passing the evolved gas through 2 4 K HgI solution, a reddish brown precipitate E is formed. Identify A, B, C, D and E and write the chemical equations for the reactions involved.
questions.answer: The following reactions are: 4 2 2 2 2 4 2 2 4 4 4 2 4NH Cl + K Cr O + 6H SO 2CrO Cl + 4NH HSO + 2KHSO + 3H O → 2 2 4 CrOCl + 2NaOH Na CrO + 2HCl → 2 4 3 2 4 3 Na CrO + (CH COO) Pb PbCrO + 2CH COONa → 4 2 3 NH Cl + NaOH NaCl + H O + NH → 2 2 3 2 2 2K HgI + NH + 3KOH NH (HgO)HgI + 7KI + 2H O → According to the above reaction, the compound A is 4 NH Cl, compound B is 2 2 CrO Cl , compound C is 2 4 Na CrO , compound D is 4 PbCrO and compound E is 2 NH (HgO)HgI which is also known as Millon’s base.

questions.question: Answer the following: (a) Describe the preparation of potassium dichromate 2 2 7 (K Cr O ) . Write the chemical equations of the reactions involved.
questions.answer: Potassium dichromate is manufactured from chromite ore 2 3 (FeO.Cr O ) in the following steps: Class 12 Chemistry 23 Step 1: Chromite ore is roasted into sodium chromate: 2 3 2 3 2 2 3 2 4 2 4FeO.Cr O 8Na CO 7O 2Fe O 8Na CrO 8CO + + → + + Step 2: Sodium chromate is converted into sodium dichromate: 2 4 2 4 2 2 7 2 4 2 2Na CrO H SO Na Cr O Na SO H O + → + + Step 3: Sodium dichromate converted to potassium dichromate: 2 2 7 2 2 7 Na Cr O 2KCl K Cr O 2NaCl + → + (b) “The chromates and dichromates are interconvertible by the change in pH of medium.” Why? Give chemical equations in favour of your answer. The equilibrium reaction of dichromate and chromate ions is given as: 2 2 + 2 7 2 4 Cr O H O 2CrO 2H − − + + In the above reaction, dichromate ions 2 2 7 (Cr O ) −give orange colour and chromate ions 2 4 (CrO ) − give yellow colour. So, when the concentration of + H ions is increased in the solution, the pH decreases, that means that pH of the solution is acidic and the equilibrium of the reaction shifts towards left side and it gives orange coloured dichromate ions. When the concentration of OH− ions is increased in the solution, the pH of the solution increases and the equilibrium of the reaction shifts towards right side and it gives yellow coloured chromate ions.

questions.question: Explain giving reasons: (a) Transition metals are less reactive than the alkali metals and alkaline earth metals.
questions.answer: Because of their high ionisation potential and melting temperature, transition metals are less reactive than alkali metals. Electrons occupy the same shell or orbital as they move from left to right on the periodic table, with the alkali metals having the least full outermost shells, one electron, implying that they have fewer protons. As a result, they have lower nucleus attraction, whereas comparable Class 12 Chemistry 24 transition metals of the same period have more protons interacting with electrons at the same distance from the nucleus as alkali metals. (b) o 2 + E (Cu / Cu) has positive value In comparison to + 2 H /H , a positive result for 2 + Cu /Cushows that this reduction occurs quickly. As a result, it is a more powerful oxidising agent, with the redox couple 2 + Cu /Cu being more powerful than + 2 H /H . It also means that Cu is unable to convert + H ions in an acid to 2 H . (c) Elements in the middle of transition series have higher melting points. The higher melting point of transition metals is due to the interatomic metallic bonding involving a bigger number of (n 1)d − electrons in addition to the ns electrons. Except for anomalous values of manganese and technetium, the melting points of these metals climb to a maximum at 5 d as the atomic number increases during a period of 3d series. (d) The decrease in atomic size of transition elements in a series is very small. The outer electrons have a higher nuclear charge due to the weak shielding effect of d-electrons. Because they have a higher effective nuclear charge, their size reduces somewhat, resulting in less fluctuation in transition element size.

questions.question: Answer the following: (a) Compare the chemistry of the actinoids with that of lanthanoids with reference to:
questions.answer: (a) (i) Due to small size and high ionic charge / availability of d-orbitals. (ii) Higher is the oxidation state, higher is the acidic character / as the oxidation state of a metal increases, ionic character decreases. (iii) Because Mn2+ has d5 as a stable configuration whereas Cr3+ is more stable due to stable t3 2g. 1 (b) Similarity–Both are stable in +3 oxidation state/ both show contraction/irregular electronic configuration (or any other suitable similarity). Difference– actinoids are radioactive and lanthanoids are not / actinoids show wide range of oxidation states but lanthanoids don’t (or any other correct difference). OR (a) (i) In p-block elements the difference in oxidation state is 2 and in transition metals the difference is 1. (ii) Cu+ , due to disproportionation reaction / low hydration enthalpy. ½ + ½ (iii) Due to formation of chromate ion / CrO4 2– ion, which is yellow in colour. (b) Actinoids are radioactive , actinoids show wide range of oxidation states. 1 + 1 Detailed Answer: (a) (i) Transition metals form large number of complexes due to following reasons: a. Small size of metal ion b. High charge density c. Availability of empty d-orbitals (ii) In the lowest oxides of transition metals, the metal atom has low oxidation state. This means some of the valence electrons of metal atom are not involved in the bonding. So, these electrons are not available for donation. Hence, they are basic in nature. However, in the higher oxides of transition metals, the metal atom has high oxidation state. This means all of the valence electrons of metal atom are involved in the bonding. So, these electrons are not available for donation. Also, these metal ions have high effective nuclear charge. As a result, they can accept electrons. Hence, they are acidic in nature. (iii) Mn2+ exists in half-filled d5 state which is very stable while Mn3+ is d4 which is not so stable. Conversion from d4 to d5 will be quick and have negative ΔG value. Hence, because the stability factor of the E° value is high for this process. While Cr3+ is d3 is half-filled (t3 2g) is stable in nature and Cr2+ is d4 has one extra electron which it would like to donate to attain the stable half-filled (t3 2g) configuration. Hence, for the process Cr3+ to Cr2+, the value of E° is less. (b) Similarities between lanthanoid and actinoid: (i) They have prominent oxidation states of +3. (ii) They are electropositive. (iii) They show magnetic properties Differences between lanthanoid and actinoid : (i) Lanthanoids are involved in filling of 4f-orbitals, actinoids are involved in filling of 5f-orbitals. (ii) Binding energy of 4f-orbitals or lanthanoids is less than 5f-orbitals or actinoids. OR (a) (i) Variable oxidation states shown by transition element can differ by one unit while oxidation state shown by non- transition elements differ by two units (sometimes due to inert pair effect). For example, oxidation state of S = – 2, – 4, – 6 Oxidation state of Co= + 2, + 3, + 4 (ii) Cu+ is unstable in aqueous solution while Cu2+ is more stable. Stability in aqueous medium depends upon the hydration energy, although it will require energy to remove one electron from Cu+ to convert it into Cu2+ but that is compensated by high hydration energy of Cu2+. Hence Cu2+ is more stable than that of Cu+. (iii) The change in colour is due to the formation of chromate ion. Cr2O2 7 – + H2O 2Cr2O2 – +2H+ (b) The reasons for this are as follows : (i) Actinoids display a large number of oxidation states while lanthanoids primarily show only three oxidation states. (ii) Actinoids are radioactive in nature.

questions.question: Electronic configuration
questions.answer: Electronic configuration: The electrical configuration of lanthanoids is [ ] 1 14 0 1 2 Xe 4f 5d 6s − − . The electronic configuration of actinoids is [ ] 1 14 0 1 2 Rn 5f 6d 7s − − .

questions.question: Oxidation states Class 12 Chemistry
questions.answer: Oxidation states: Due to the wide energy difference between the 4f and 5d subshells, lanthanoids have 2 + and 4 + oxidation states in addition to the 3 + oxidation state. Due of the narrow energy difference between the5f and 6d subshells, actinoids have a high variety of oxidation states.

questions.question: Chemical reactivity.
questions.answer: Chemical reactivity: Lanthanoids with high electropositivity exhibit chemical reactivity that is almost identical. Actinoids (electropositive and highly reactive) are more reactive than lanthanides (especially in finely split form). (b) How would you account for the following:

questions.question: Of the 4 d species, 2 + Cr is strongly reducing while 3 + Mn is strongly oxidising.
questions.answer: The usual electrode potential for 3 + 2 + Cr /Cr is( 0.41V) − , while 3 + 2 + Mn /Mn is ( 1.57V) + . 2 + Cr is a reducing agent that can undergo oxidation. 3 + Mn is an oxidising agent that can undergo reduction.

questions.question: The lowest oxide of a transition metal is basic whereas highest is amphoteric or acidic.
questions.answer: Some of the valence electrons of the metal atom are not engaged in bonding in the low oxidation state of the metal. As a result, it has the ability to transfer electrons and act as a base. Valence electrons, on the other hand, are engaged in bonding and are not available in higher oxidation states of the metal. Instead, because the effective nuclear charge is large, it may take electrons and act like an acid.

questions.question: Answer the following: (a) What is meant by disproportionation of an oxidation state. Give one example.
questions.answer: Disproportionation occurs when a state becomes less stable in comparison to two different oxidation states, one lower and one higher. The example for the disproportionation of oxidation state is: Class 12 Chemistry 26 VI 2 VII IV 4 4 2 2 3 H Mn O 4H 2Mn O Mn O 2 O − + − + → + + (b) Explain why europium (II) is more stable than Ce(II)? The effective nuclear charge increases as we travel from left to right, causing lanthanide contraction. The dominance of the inert pair effect increases. Eu (II) is more stable than Ce (II)due to its lower oxidation property.

questions.question: Answer the following: (a) For 2 + M / M and 3 + 2 + M / M systems, the o E values for some metals are as follows: 2 + Cr / Cr = -0.9V and 3 + 2 + Cr / Cr = -0.4V \ 2 + Mn / Mn = -1.2 V and 3 + 2 + Mn / Mn = + 1.5V , 2 + Fe / Fe = -0.4V and 3 + 2 + Fe / Fe = + 0.8V Use this data to comment upon: (i) The stability of 3+ Fe in acid solution as compared to that of 3+ Cr and 3+ Mn
questions.answer: The stability of 3 + Fe in acid solution as compared to that of 3 + Cr and 3 + Mn Because the reduction potential of 3 + 2 + Cr /Cr is negative, Cr (III) is the most stable compound because it cannot be reduced to Cr (II). 3 + 2 + Mn /Mn has a significant positive value, and Mn (III) is the least stable since it may be converted to Mn (II) quickly. 3 + 2 + Fe /Fe has a modest positive value, and Fe (III) is more stable than Mn (III) , but not as much as Cr (III). (ii) The ease with which iron can be oxidised as compared to a similar process for either chromium or manganese. Ans: 2 + Mn /Mn has the highest negative reduction potential and is the easiest to oxidise. Mn Cr F e > > is the order of oxidation easiness. (b) How is the variability in oxidation states of transition metals different from that of the non-transition metals? Illustrate with examples. Class 12 Chemistry 27 The involvement of ( ) n 1 d − orbitals and ns orbitals in transition elements causes oxidation state fluctuation. By a factor of one, the oxidation states vary. As a result, vanadium has oxidation states of + 2, + 3, + 4, + 5, + 6, and + 7 , whereas manganese has oxidation states of + 2, + 3, + 4, + 5, + 6, and + 7 . The varied oxidation states indicated by some p-block elements, on the other hand, differ by two units. Tin has oxidation states of 2 + and 4 + , while indium has oxidation levels of 1 + and 3 + .

questions.question: Define the term coordination compound?
Write the names of counter ions in (i) Hg [Co (SCN)4] and (ii) [Pt(NH3)4] Cl2.
questions.answer: The IUPAC name of the given compound is 2 – Butylamine. questions.answer: wer the following: (i) Write the element which shows maximum number of oxidation states. Give reason. (ii) Which element has the highest m.p. ? (iii) Which element shows only + 3 oxidation state ? (iv) Which element is a strong oxidizing agent in + 3 oxidation state and why ? (a) (i) Ability of oxygen to form multiple bond with Mn metal. (ii) Cr2+ is oxidized to Cr3+ which has stable d3 / t3 2g orbital configuration. (iii) Cu2+ has unpaired electron while Zn2+ has no unpaired electron. (b) (i) 2MnO2 + 4KOH + O2 ∆ → 2K2MnO4 + 2H2O (ii) Cr2O7 2– + 14H+ + 6I– → 2Cr3+ + 7H2O + 3I2 (balanced equation is required) 1 OR (i) Mn. It has maximum unpaired electrons. ½ + 1 (ii) Cr (iii) Sc (iv) Manganese. Mn3+ to Mn2+ results in the stable half filled (d5) configuration. ½ + 1 Detailed Answer: (a) (i) Mn exhibits highest oxidation states (upto + 7) in the oxides, for example Mn2O7 in comparison to Mn fluorides, MnF4 as oxygen is able to form multiple bond with metal. And also fluorine does not have d-orbital. (ii) Cr2+ is a strong reducing agent as its electrode potential is more negative. Also, it can loose on electron to become Cr3+ which has half-filled d-orbital which is its stable state. (iii) Electronic configuration : 29Cu = [Ar] 3d10 4s1 Cu2+ = [Ar] 3d9 30Zn = [Ar] 3d10 4s2 Zn2+ = [Ar] 3d10 (b) (i) 2MnO2 + 4KOH + O2 ∆ → Manganese Potassium dioxide hydroxide 2K2MnO4 + 2H2O Potassium manganate (ii) Cr2O7 2– + 14H+ + 6I– → 2Cr3++ 3I2 + 7H2O OR (i) Manganese exhibits greatest number of oxidation states as it is able to acquire half filled to completely filled configurations. Thus Mn can loose or share electrons from both the orbitals. (ii) Cr has the highest melting point. As the number unpaired electrons increases upto d5 configuration, it results in the increase in the strength of metallic bonds. To break the metallic bond, significant energy is required thus Cr with highest number of unpaired electrons (6) has the highest melting point. (iii) Sc shows only +3 oxidation state as if has too few electrons to lose thus, looses one electron from 3d-electron to attain + 3 oxidation state. (iv) Co is the strongest oxidising agent as the E value for the redox couple M3+ /M2+ is highest.

questions.answer: (i) The IUPAC name of [Cr(NH3)4 Cl2]Cl is Tetraam minedichlorochromium(III) chloride. (ii) The isomerism exhibited by this complex is the optical isomerism. (iii) The oxidation state of Ni in [NiCl4]2– is +2. Since chloride is a weak field ligand, it does not cause electron pairing up in opposition to Hund's maximum multiplicity rule. As a result, Ni's valence d-orbitals contain two unpaired electrons, giving the complex its paramagnetic properties. On the other hand, carbonyl is a strong field ligand and causes electron partnering up in opposition to Hund's maximisation rule. As a result, there are no unpaired electrons and the complex is hence diamagnetic.
